text: The surgeon performs a right hemicolectomy, targeting the hepatic flexure. Bipolar forceps, scissors, and a tip-up clamp are utilized with the robotic arms, while the camera provides a clear view. The right colon is lifted to expose the alocholic vessels. The heliocolic vessels are identified, and dissection begins medially to laterally. The aneocolic vein and artery are clipped with amalocs and cut at their origin using the clip applier in the third robotic arm.

The surgeon continues the medial to lateral dissection, placing a swab cranially after completing the medial dissection. The mesentery of the distal ileum is divided using a robotic endo-vessel sealer in the third arm. Lateral dissection is performed along the embryologic plane.

The surgeon divides adhesions between the omentum and the transverse colon, revealing a tattoo marking on the colon. The omentum is further divided, and the swab placed after the medial dissection is visible, protecting the duodenum and surrounding vessels.

The surgeon completes the lateral dissection and takes down the hepatic flexure. The mesocolon is transected using a vessel sealer. During the DII colectomy, the ileocolic artery and vein are sectioned at their origin at the superior mesenteric vein and artery. The right branch of the middle colic vein and artery are also sectioned at their origin, while the right colic artery and vein are absent.
The surgeon completes the dissection and divides the transverse colon and terminal ileum using an endostepler, which is positioned in the third robotic arm.

The surgeon performs dissection and release of the second adrenal portion to expose the renal pedicle. The renal pedicle is dissected to identify the right renal artery, which is prepared for clamping. The inferior pole of the kidney is exposed, and the margins of the lesion are marked with diatomy. Subsequently, the renal artery is clamped, initiating warm ischemia.
The surgeon performs a tumorectomy, ensuring macroscopically healthy tissue margins are maintained. A hemostatic continuous barbed suture is applied using Hemolox to minimize kidney worm ischemia.

The surgeon removes the arterial clamp, checks for kidney reperfusion and absence of active bleeding, then removes the specimen and places it in a bag. A hemostatic agent is applied at the tumorectomy site.
The surgeon performs a side-to-side isoperistaltic ileocolic anastomosis, using indocyanine green to visually assess the vascularization of the ileum and colon involved in the anastomosis.

The surgeon uses bipolar forceps in the first robotic arm, a needle holder in the third, and a tip-up instrument in the fourth, while the camera is positioned in the second arm. A posterior reinforcement is performed using a running three-hole absorbable suture, followed by a barber's suture.

After completing the posterior suture, the surgeon creates two enterotomies. The assistant uses a suction device to clear the surgical field. A staple side-to-side anastomosis is performed using the endostaple placed in the third robotic arm.

The surgeon closes the defect using single absorbable three-hole wiper stitches, while the assistant uses scissors. An anterior reinforcement is performed with a running three-hole absorbable barbed suture, and the epiprone is applied to cover the anastomosis.
Both specimens are extracted from the surgical field via a funnestine incision, each placed in separate endo bags.
